symbols: angels with trumpets — the last trumpet call that summons the dead to rise
bodies emerging from earth — the general resurrection at the end of time
skeletons with flesh reconstituting — the process of bodily resurrection as described by Ezekiel
banners held by angels — the triumph of Christ over death
naked figures — humanity stripped of all pretense before the judgment
description: A fresco within an arched frame showing two large angels at the top blowing long trumpets and holding banners. Below them, numerous nude human figures emerge from the earth in various stages of reconstitution — some are still skeletal, others have partial flesh, while others are fully formed and standing upright. The scene is densely populated with rising bodies, some helping each other up, others still entangled with the earth. The color palette is warm with flesh tones against a lighter background.
theological_description: Signorelli's Resurrection of the Dead in Orvieto Cathedral is one of the most powerful visual meditations on the doctrine of bodily resurrection in Christian art. The trumpeting angels fulfill Paul's prophecy: 'The Lord himself will descend from heaven with a cry of command, with the voice of an archangel, and with the sound of the trumpet of God. And the dead in Christ will rise first' (1 Thessalonians 4:16). The detailed depiction of bodies in various stages of reconstitution — from bare skeleton to fully fleshed — visually narrates the process described in Ezekiel's vision of the valley of dry bones: 'I will cause breath to enter you, and you shall live. I will lay sinews upon you, and will cause flesh to come upon you' (Ezekiel 37:5-6). The bodily resurrection was a central and contested doctrine of the early Church; Paul argued for it passionately: 'If there is no resurrection of the dead, then not even Christ has been raised' (1 Corinthians 15:13). Signorelli's fresco insists on the physical, bodily nature of resurrection — not a mere spiritual survival but a complete reconstitution of the whole person, body and soul, for eternal life or judgment.

scripture_refs.reason: The Lord descending with trumpet call and the dead in Christ rising — the exact scene depicted
Dry bones reconstituting with sinew, flesh, and skin — the visual process shown in the fresco
At the last trumpet the dead will be raised imperishable — Paul's resurrection theology
The sea, death, and Hades giving up their dead for judgment
All who are in their graves will hear his voice and come out — Jesus's teaching on general resurrection
